questions.question: India gained independence from British rule on which date in 1947?
questions.explanation: India gained independence from British rule on August 15, 1947, following decades of nonviolent resistance led by Mahatma Gandhi and the Indian National Congress. Jawaharlal Nehru became India's first Prime Minister. The same day, Pakistan was created as a separate Muslim-majority state — leading to one of history's largest mass migrations and significant sectarian violence. August 15 is celebrated as India's Independence Day.
questions.memory_hook: India independent August 15, 1947. Nehru gave the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at midnight. Same day Pakistan was born — the subcontinent split in two, causing 10-20 million people to flee in both directions and between 200,000 and 2 million killed in the chaos.

questions.question: The Battle of Waterloo in 1815 ended whose rule over much of Europe?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Waterloo (June 18, 1815) in present-day Belgium was Napoleon's final defeat. He was defeated by the Duke of Wellington's allied forces and Prussian troops. Napoleon abdicated four days later and was exiled to Saint Helena island in the South Atlantic, where he died in 1821. The battle permanently ended French domination of Europe and established the peace settlement of the Congress of Vienna.
questions.memory_hook: Waterloo 1815 = Napoleon's final defeat. He was exiled to Saint Helena in the South Atlantic — as far from Europe as possible. He died there 6 years later. The battle lasted one day and ended 20 years of Napoleonic wars. ABBA later immortalized the defeat in a song.

questions.question: What was the name of the first successful permanent English settlement in North America, founded in 1607?
questions.explanation: Jamestown, Virginia, was founded on May 14, 1607, by the Virginia Company of England. It was the first permanent English settlement in the Americas. The colony struggled initially with disease, starvation, and conflict with the Powhatan Confederacy. Tobacco cultivation eventually made it economically viable. The famous Pocahontas was the daughter of Powhatan chief Wahunsenacah.
questions.memory_hook: Jamestown 1607 = first permanent English settlement in America. It almost failed — 'the starving time' winter of 1609-10 killed 80% of colonists. Tobacco saved it economically. Pocahontas was real — and her story is much more complicated and sadder than the Disney version.

questions.question: In which Japanese martial art do competitors earn points by throwing, pinning, or forcing their opponent to submit — with a perfect throw earning an 'ippon' and immediate victory?
questions.explanation: Judo, meaning 'gentle way,' was developed by Jigoro Kano in Japan in 1882. An ippon (full point) is awarded for a clean throw landing the opponent on their back, a 25-second pin, or a submission. Judo was the first Asian martial art to become an Olympic sport, debuting at the 1964 Tokyo Games.
questions.memory_hook: Judo: 'gentle way,' ippon = instant win. Olympic since 1964 Tokyo.

questions.question: In volleyball, what is a 'dig'?
options: A defensive pass of a spiked ball
questions.answer: A defensive pass of a spiked ball
questions.explanation: A dig in volleyball is a defensive technique to pass a ball that has been attacked (usually spiked) by the opposing team, typically performed with forearm passing to keep the ball alive. The goal is to prevent the ball from hitting the floor when the opponent attacks. A successful dig allows the team to transition from defense to offense. It requires quick reflexes and good anticipation.
questions.memory_hook: Dig = defensive forearm pass of a spike. You dig out a ball that's been spiked at you. Good liberos (the defensive specialists) are defined by their digging ability — diving across the floor to save balls that look like certain points for the other team.

questions.question: Which 1995 Pixar film was the first feature-length computer-animated movie?
questions.explanation: Toy Story (1995), directed by John Lasseter at Pixar Animation Studios, was the world's first feature-length computer-animated film. Made with groundbreaking CGI technology, it grossed $373 million on a $30 million budget. It launched a franchise of four films and changed animation forever. Tom Hanks voiced Woody; Tim Allen voiced Buzz Lightyear. Disney acquired Pixar in 2006 for $7.4 billion.
questions.memory_hook: Toy Story = first feature CGI film ever. 1995. $30 million budget, $373 million gross. Changed animation permanently — traditional hand-drawn Disney films declined; CGI took over. Tom Hanks as Woody, Tim Allen as Buzz. The friendship between those two characters became Pixar's template for every film after.

questions.question: The Eagles' 'Hotel California' album was released in which year?
questions.explanation: Hotel California was released by the Eagles on February 22, 1977. The album went to number one in the US, Australia, and Canada, and has sold over 32 million copies worldwide. Beyond the title track, it features 'Life in the Fast Lane' and 'New Kid in Town.' The album's themes of hedonism and disillusionment in the California music industry captured a cultural moment.
questions.memory_hook: Hotel California = Eagles, 1977. 32 million copies sold. 'You can check out any time you like, but you can never leave.' Don Felder wrote the iconic guitar riff sitting in his Malibu house. Don Henley wrote the lyrics about excess in the California music scene. They barely got along while making it.

questions.question: Grant Wood's 'American Gothic' (1930) — the stern farmer and woman standing before a white house — is one of America's most parodied images. What was Wood's original intention?
options: Wood intended it as a proud celebration of Midwestern rural values and the Protestant work ethic that built America — a patriotic portrait of agrarian virtue

Wood intended it as a gentle satire of what he called 'the narrow-faced, pinch-lipped, rigidly moral' type he associated with small-town Midwestern puritanism — though most Iowans took it as a compliment
Wood created it as a direct critique of the economic inequality exposed by the Great Depression, with the pitchfork representing agricultural labor exploited by absentee landlords
Wood's intention was entirely aesthetic — he discovered the house (now called 'the American Gothic House') and simply needed two human figures to show scale
questions.answer: Wood intended it as a gentle satire of what he called 'the narrow-faced, pinch-lipped, rigidly moral' type he associated with small-town Midwestern puritanism — though most Iowans took it as a compliment
questions.explanation: Wood was inspired by a Carpenter Gothic house in Eldon, Iowa and painted his sister Nan and his dentist Dr. Byron McKeeby as the models — not a married couple but a father and daughter, as Wood later clarified. Iowans initially complained the painting made them look 'pinched, grim, and repressed,' but it was quickly adopted as a symbol of American frontier resilience. The models — Nan and McKeeby — became briefly famous. The painting was purchased by the Art Institute of Chicago from Wood for $300 in 1930.
questions.memory_hook: American Gothic's models were Wood's sister and his dentist — not a couple but a father-daughter pair. Wood intended mild satire of Midwestern puritanism; Iowans saw patriotic pride. The Art Institute of Chicago bought it for $300.

questions.question: Which is the only continent with land in all four hemispheres?
questions.explanation: Africa is the only continent with land in all four hemispheres: Northern (most of Africa), Southern (southern tip), Eastern (most of Africa), and Western (western coast from Morocco to Senegal). It is the only continent centered near the prime meridian and the equator simultaneously. The equator runs through the middle of Africa, dividing it roughly in half.
questions.memory_hook: Africa = all four hemispheres. It's essentially centered on the intersection of the equator and prime meridian. No other continent is so geographically centered on Earth. That centrality made Africa the cradle of human civilization and the crossroads of ancient trade.

questions.question: What is the name of the phenomenon where light bends as it passes from one medium to another?
questions.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light (or other waves) when they pass from one medium to another with different optical density. A straw in a glass of water appears bent because light travels slower in water than in air, causing it to change direction at the surface. Refraction explains how lenses work, how prisms split white light into colors, and why the sky appears to shift color at sunrise and sunset.
questions.memory_hook: Refraction = light bends when changing mediums. A straw looks bent in water because of refraction. A prism splits white light into a rainbow by refracting different wavelengths at different angles. Your glasses or contact lenses work entirely because of controlled refraction.

questions.question: The Sea of Japan lies between Japan and the Korean Peninsula. By what name do South Korea and Japan disagree on what this body of water should be called?
options: Korea calls it the East Sea; both names are used
Both agree it should be the Pacific Sea
Japan insists on calling it the Korea Sea
The UN has ruled it must be called the Pacific Ocean
questions.answer: Korea calls it the East Sea; both names are used
questions.explanation: South Korea calls the body of water the 'East Sea' (동해, Donghae), while Japan calls it the 'Sea of Japan.' North Korea calls it the 'East Sea of Korea.' This naming dispute has geopolitical dimensions — South Korea argues the 'Sea of Japan' name was imposed during Japanese colonization (1910-1945). International organizations have begun using both names. Historically, European maps used various names; 'Sea of Japan' became dominant in the 19th century.
questions.memory_hook: Sea of Japan vs. East Sea: Japan and Korea have a naming dispute. Korea says 'Sea of Japan' was imposed during colonization (1910-1945). Now many international bodies use both names. Geography and history intertwined — even naming a sea is political.

questions.question: What is a DNA mutation, and what are three things that can cause one?
options: An intentional change cells make to adapt to stress
A change in the DNA sequence, caused by radiation, chemical exposure, or copying errors
The conversion of RNA back into DNA
questions.answer: A change in the DNA sequence, caused by radiation, chemical exposure, or copying errors
questions.explanation: A DNA mutation is any permanent change in the nucleotide sequence of DNA. Mutations can be caused by: radiation (UV light, X-rays, nuclear radiation), chemical mutagens (tobacco smoke, certain industrial chemicals), or spontaneous errors during DNA replication. Most mutations are either harmless or repaired by cellular machinery. Some cause cancer by disrupting genes that control cell growth. Occasional mutations in germline cells (sperm/eggs) are inherited by offspring and drive evolution — the raw material of natural selection.
questions.memory_hook: DNA mutation = change in the DNA sequence. Caused by UV radiation, chemical mutagens, or copying errors. Most are repaired or harmless. Cancer mutations = uncontrolled cell division. Germline mutations are inherited, drive evolution. Your DNA is copied billions of times; even with error-checking, some mutations get through.
